Klarna fired 700 people, ditched Salesforce, and bet everything on OpenAI. Then they started hiring humans back.

What did not change? Their data layer. Neo4j graph database. The internal tech stack that maps customer relationships, transaction patterns, and risk signals into a shared structure. That stayed. The model was the experiment. The ontology was the investment.

This keeps happening.

Perplexity published their 2025 enterprise data last month. 43.6% of organizations used more than one AI model during the year. In January, two models held 90% of usage. By December, four models each held over 10%. The leading model captured just 23%.

Models are rotating through enterprises like contractors. The data layer is the full-time hire.

The most instructive example right now is the Pentagon's Maven Smart System, which just became a $13 billion program of record. Fireship broke down the open-source equivalent of its architecture this week. Five layers:

Ingestion (Kafka). Extraction (Spark). Ontology (Neo4j). Rules (Open Policy Agent). Agents (MCP and frontier models).

The model sits at Layer 5. Anthropic refused to remove safety guardrails on autonomous weapons and mass surveillance. Hegseth banned Claude from all DoD contracts. The Pentagon is now swapping in other models. It will take months to rebuild the cognitive layer. The ontology, the ingestion pipelines, the policy engine? Not going anywhere. They are the system.

Same pattern everywhere. Shopify's competitive moat is not which model powers their AI assistant. It is 20 years of commerce data, 130+ behavioral signals per customer, and a structured product schema that AI agents can transact against. Their AI-driven orders grew 11x last year. The model is interchangeable. The data architecture is not.

Every company building "AI-powered" anything should be asking one question: what are your layers 1 through 4?

If you are starting at the model and hoping it does the rest, you are building on sand. The organizations that compound from here are the ones with the best ontology. The best data structure. The best understanding of what their information actually means and how it connects.

Models are commoditizing fast. Ontologies are not.

On March 30, California Governor Newsom signed Executive Order N-5-26, requiring AI companies seeking state contracts to certify their policies on bias prevention, civil rights protections, and privacy safeguards.

This came less than four months after Trump's December executive order directing the Attorney General to create a task force specifically to challenge state AI laws, and conditioning federal funding on states not enacting "onerous" AI regulations.

Two levels of government are now moving in opposite directions on AI governance. And every enterprise operating across state lines is caught in the middle.

The regulatory count is already significant. California has over 20 AI laws effective in 2026, covering transparency, content safety, algorithmic pricing, and healthcare AI. Colorado's AI Act goes live June 30, 2026, requiring annual impact assessments for high-risk AI systems. Illinois bans AI hiring discrimination. New York City mandates annual bias audits for automated employment tools. Texas's RAIGA took effect January 1. And none of these are harmonized with each other.

The enterprises I talk to aren't debating whether AI will be regulated. They're trying to figure out how to comply with five different regulatory frameworks simultaneously, while the federal government actively tries to preempt some of them.

This is why governance is becoming an infrastructure decision, not a compliance exercise. If your governance model requires manual reconfiguration every time a new state law takes effect, it doesn't scale. The organizations getting ahead of this are building governance at the infrastructure layer, where policies can be updated, enforced, and audited centrally regardless of which jurisdiction applies.

That's how Trussed is architected. One governance layer that enforces policy across every AI interaction, adaptable to any regulatory framework, without rebuilding compliance for each new requirement.

If you operate across multiple states, how many different AI governance standards apply to your organization today? And can your current governance model enforce all of them at once?

Sources:
DLA Piper analysis of Executive Order N-5-26: https://lnkd.in/gt3b7_Pk
King and Spalding analysis of the federal vs. state tension: https://lnkd.in/gdjY3rYB

Most AI spending can't be tied to a business outcome. Close that gap, and your team gets bigger without hiring.

I made that argument eleven days ago. Here's what the other side looks like in practice.

I'm running a job search and a consulting practice on a single agentic system I built myself using Claude Code. 165 companies tracked, 8,000+ contacts, 38 opportunities, 550 logged interactions, 19 interviews prepped with tailored research, 567 content ideas captured, 36 posts published. One person. No coordinators, no research assistants, no scheduling help.

That's not a productivity gain. It's a different operating model.

I saw an early version of this years ago at a previous employer. We had hundreds of thousands of contracts governing what data we could and couldn't use. The manual review path was measured in years of specialist work. AI read them in weeks and surfaced the usage rules. I wrote last week about why that story mattered for governance. The other lesson I took from it was about team capacity. We had just done something that would have been impossible at human speed.

Someone I spoke with recently who runs a data organization shared a similar shift. His team had a predictive model that only got refreshed once a year, because refreshing it took two specialist data scientists about a person-month of work. The model ran stale for most of the year. One of his practitioners rebuilt it in an agentic coding environment, set it to refresh continuously, and generated an interactive web presentation in the same workflow. The model is now accurate year-round, and the two specialists are free to take on problems the organization could never afford to attempt before.

Most organizations are nowhere near this. They've bolted AI features onto every tool they already own, so a typical analyst now juggles dozens of interfaces, each with its own AI chat, none of them aware of the others. Vendors call that AI-enabled. It doesn't multiply anything. It adds windows to manage.

The multiplier comes from clear outcomes and clear ownership. When one person is accountable for a measurable business result, the system gets designed backward from that result. Agents handle the specialist work. Domain expertise gets wrapped into reusable skills the whole team can run. The hand-offs between narrow roles disappear, because those hand-offs were mostly where accountability used to leak. That's a leadership decision, not a procurement one.

That redesign is how the team gets bigger without the org chart changing.

DM me 'STRATEGY' if you're mapping AI into your team operating model.

You're losing OPT candidates because you didn't track the 150-day unemployment window.

An international student graduates. They're on Optional Practical Training (OPT), a 12-month work permit that's the most cost-effective hiring window for staffing firms. You place them on day 45. They work for 4 months. Client's budget cuts, and the placement ends.

Now they're in unemployment. You think nothing of it. They're on their own for a few weeks while you find the next role.

But OPT has a hard rule: 150 days of unemployment during the authorization period and they lose the entire remaining permit. If they were on month 8 of 12 when the placement ended, those weeks suddenly matter.

Most staffing firms don't track this. They don't even know when their OPT candidate hit day 140. And suddenly that candidate's legally required to leave the U.S. Or worse—they're on bench, and nobody realized they've used up their grace period.

Here's what changes when you have OPT employment tracking in ElevateStaffing.ai:

Every day counts. When an OPT candidate enters unemployment, a timer starts automatically. You see how many days they can afford to be unplaced. Placements are prioritized to keep them under the 150-day threshold. When they're getting close, alerts trigger your desk. The candidate doesn't lose status due to your system's blindness.

And if you do place them on H-1B, you have the employment history documented already—no scrambling for proof of OPT work.

It's not complicated. It's just tracked.

The CEO’s Final Pivot: From People-Scale to Outcome-Elasticity
​The traditional service-based model is broken. In a market where 91% of enterprises have moved AI agents into production, the "Billable Hour" is no longer an asset; it is a weight around your neck. The firms currently being valued at 25x multiples are not the ones with the most employees, but the ones with the most Sovereign Intelligence. 
 
​1. The Death of "Middle-Office" Overhead
​In 2026, 80% of your multi-step workflows data analysis, reporting, and research can be handled by Agentic Orchestrators. The Reality: Firms like Harvey and Legora are hitting $10B+ valuations because they aren't selling software; they are selling the elimination of labor.
​Your Move: Shift your budget from hiring "Generalists" to building Custom Agentic Chains. If a process in your company can be described, it can be automated. If it can be automated, it should be an Internal Asset, not a human expense.

​2. Alternative Fee Arrangements (AFA) or Irrelevance
​Clients are no longer asking for your hourly rates; they are writing Fixed-Price Performance Contracts.  The Arbitrage: If you automate a task that used to take 100 hours, you do not pass that 99% time saving to the client. You price based on the Risk Mitigated and the Value Created.  The Goal: Capture the "Efficiency Alpha." The margin created by AI belongs to your shareholders, not your client’s procurement department.

​3. The "Junior-to-Verifier" Talent Shift
​The most expensive part of your business is the "Learning Curve" of junior talent.
​The Solution: 2026 is the year of the "Super-Junior." AI agents handle the execution, while your junior staff are trained as Auditors and Directors.  The Impact: This compresses your time-to-value for new hires from 18 months to 18 days. You aren't scaling people; you are scaling Expertise.

📢 Research Positions in Bristol, UK 🇬🇧 

Bristol is inviting you to apply to undertake postdoctoral research in the group of Professor Craig Butts, developing and applying workflows for elucidation of 3D chemical structure based on NMR spectroscopic data, funded by an EPSRC programme in collaboration with Professor Andre Cobb at Kings College London. We are one of the leading groups in the UK and internationally in this field and are involved in several research collaborations, including projects with industry (AstraZeneca, Evotec and Genentech) and EPSRC-funded programmes of research, working with synthetic chemists to test our machine learning systems against challenging real world examples. The role holder will lead on a programme of computation and experimental measurement of NMR spectroscopic data and also liaise with our machine learning and data science teams to explore new frontiers in multi-modal automated structure determination. and will assist Professor Butts in mentoring his research group, coordinating day-to-day activities and providing support for post-graduate and undergraduate researchers.

Job Responsibilities:
*- Developing and applying NMR experimental methods to determining 3D molecular structure
*- Conduct computational studies of 3D structures to compare to the experimental NMR data
*- Working with machine learning specialists in our team to develop experimental testing sets for their AI tools for 3D molecular structure elucidation, in particular synthetic catalysts or natural products of unknown conformation and constitution.
*- Designing and helping prepare manuscripts in Professor Butts’ experimental NMR sub-group.
*- Supporting the development of grant funding applications around 3D molecular structure elucidation.

Job Requirements:
*- You hold a PhD (or are in final stages of preparing to submit for a PhD) in Chemistry and/or physical sciences.
*- Have experience in using advanced NMR tools, such as multi-dimensional NMR for 3D structure determination
*- Have an understanding of chemistry/molecular structure
*- You enjoy collaboration across experimental, computational and data science.

Contract type: Open-ended with funding until 31/12/2028

Last date to Apply: 30/04/2026

Bloor Research built an AI twin that attends meetings, ingests documents, and mimics an analyst's thought process. They've rolled it to 50 people. New hires get one as standard.

Gartner calls this mainstream. Josh Bersin calls it the superworker. Companies are already shifting to outcome-based pay, rewarding employees for value their twin generates.

This is not productivity innovation. This is liability without a legal framework.

The problem it solves is real.

Knowledge transfer at retirement. Continuity during leave. Output that doesn't stop when a person does. Legitimate.

But the model breaks the moment something goes wrong.

The twin gives bad advice. A client acts on it. The analyst is on leave. The firm says the twin operated within approved parameters. The client says they engaged a human expert.

Who owns the error?

Not the software. Not the firm. Not the analyst who trained it three months ago from a different context.

Ownership, consent, and liability for AI twins are legally undefined. Existing employment law offers no guidance. Tribunals will write the precedents. That means the first high-value failure becomes the case study every firm is cited against.

The compensation question is equally unresolved.

If outcome-based pay rewards employees for what their twin generates while they sleep, what happens when the twin outperforms the human? Does the human get paid more? Does the twin's output get attributed differently? Does the firm quietly reduce headcount because the twin covers the gap at lower cost?

Reduced hiring needs and higher bonus pools sound like wins. They are also the description of a workforce model that extracts value from individual knowledge, institutionalises it, and then renegotiates the terms.

In transformation governance, the equivalent is when a system integrator embeds institutional knowledge into their tooling, and the client only realises the dependency after the contract ends.

Same structure. Different industry.

The superworker model is not ready for enterprise deployment at scale. Not because the technology fails. Because the governance does not exist to contain what happens when it does.

Building AI twins before ownership, liability, and compensation law are defined is not innovation. It is evidence collection for future disputes.

📢 Postdoc Position in France 🇫🇷 

The ANR Diplo project aims at bridging the gap between proof theory and functional and numerical analysis, through the semantics of differential linear logic and programming languages. In particular, this project seeks to lay the foundations for a correspondence between algorithmic computation of differentials or solutions to differential equations and the logical principles at work in linear logic and associated lambda calculi.

The postdoctoral researcher will work on this project under the supervision of Marie Kerjean, and may collaborate with other members of the ANR Diplo project. If the successful candidate wishes, the results obtained may be formalised in the Math-comp Analysis library.

This is a 12-month contract, extendable to 24 months.
- Conduct research as part of the ANR Diplo project whilst developing your own research project.
- Share the knowledge acquired during the PhD with team members and acquire new knowledge.
- Engage with the Local team at LIPN and the wider national community working on proof theory, programming languages and semantics, and present your work at national and international conferences and workshops.

The postdoctoral researcher will be based at LIPN, Sorbonne Paris Nord University – Paris 13, Villetaneuse Campus. They will be provided with an office, a workstation and access to the CNRS's IT resources. Domestic and international travel expenses will be covered by the DiPLO project.

As agentic AI takes over routine development and maintenance work, companies must fundamentally redesign their technology workforce. 

The end goal is to generate real ROI from AI—but getting the formula right is complex. 
CEOs face mounting pressures: cost reduction, geopolitical risk, shifting vendor value, and new workforce dynamics. 
Success requires rebalancing three interconnected levers—hiring, internal capability building, and vendor strategy. With the right balance, companies can triple the return they get from their technology investments. CEOs who act decisively will shape long-term economics; those who wait risk being locked into inflexible structures.

1. Hire fewer, more selective technologists to maximize ROI per employee

· Demand shifts from junior developers to senior engineers and architects who can orchestrate work across humans, agents, and vendors.
· Expert technologists using AI are several times more productive than less experienced peers, with a modest pay differential relative to the output gap.
· ROI implication: Hiring for volume rather than expertise inflates costs without increasing impact. Fewer, deliberate hires deliver more value.

2. Build internal capabilities that AI cannot replace—or risk losing ROI to vendors

· Companies cannot outsource their understanding of AI’s impact on road maps, platforms, data, or architecture without losing control of value creation.
· Adopt a skills-based view. Map proficiency, identify critical talent, and invest systematically.
· ROI implication: Company’s disciplined skills approach results in tens of millions of money in new value creation over a few years.

3. Renegotiate vendor relationships for outcomes, not effort—to capture ROI directly

· Move from managed services to managed value. Link outsourced contracts to delivery speed, quality, and business impact, not head count.
· Shift from seat-based SaaS to API-first, outcome-linked models.
· ROI implication: A bank introduced AI-driven automation targets achieve a 10% productivity uplift across internal and external teams.

4. Lead change management to unlock ROI from human-agent teams

· The shift is not purely technological. Employees need support to navigate evolving roles. Without top-down change management, reskilling efforts remain ineffective.
· ROI implication: Successful companies infuse agentic AI throughout the organization to create a true hybrid workforce—turning every employee into a technologist and empowering human-agent teams to deliver AI at scale.

5. Integrate hiring, upskilling, and vendor strategy as one system to triple returns

· These levers reinforce each other. Taken together, they shape the economics of technology in an AI-first organization.
· ROI implication: With the right balance, companies can ultimately triple the return they get from their technology investments, rather than settling for short-lived efficiency gains.

Starting August 2, 2026, the strict rules for high-risk AI systems under the EU AI Act will fully apply. While the regulation entered into force in August 2024, this is the key deadline when most high-risk requirements become mandatory.

**What is considered high-risk AI?**
These are systems that can significantly impact people’s lives — especially in employment (Annex III):

🟢 CV screening and filtering
🟢 Candidate evaluation
🟢 Hiring recommendations
🟢 Automated decisions on hiring, promotion, task allocation, or termination
🟢 Employee performance monitoring

If your AI influences access to jobs — it is high-risk.

**Who does this apply to?**
The regulation is based on *where AI is used*, not where your company is registered:

🟢 SaaS products with AI features used or sold in the EU
🟢 Outsourcing teams building/supporting AI for EU clients
🟢 Companies whose customers use their product in Europe
🟢 Startups planning EU market entry
🟢 Any AI usage involving EU data or EU candidates/employees

**Key risks**

🟢 Using AI in HR processes without proper oversight
🟢 Applying third-party tools (e.g. ChatGPT) without policies and validation
🟢 Lack of technical documentation (model logic, training data, bias testing)
🟢 Fully automated decisions without human oversight
🟢 Lack of transparency: inability to explain decisions

**Fines are serious:**

🔴 Up to €35M or 7% of global turnover — for the most severe violations
🔴 Up to €15M or 3% — for breaches of high-risk requirements

This looks a lot like GDPR: many ignored it at first — until EU clients made compliance a non-negotiable contract requirement.

**What to do now (checklist)**

✔️ Inventory — identify where AI is used (products, internal processes, HR)
✔️ Risk assessment — does AI affect decisions about people? Any EU connection?
✔️ Documentation — model behavior, training data, bias testing, risk management
✔️ Human oversight — ensure final decisions are made by humans
✔️ Transparency — inform candidates and employees about AI usage
✔️ Policies — define internal rules for generative AI usage
✔️ Audit & monitoring — plan ongoing checks and post-deployment monitoring

The earlier you start, the smoother August 2026 will be. Many EU clients are already including AI Act compliance in contracts.

If your company works with the EU — this is no longer “something that might affect you.” It already does.

Those who make compliance part of their product will gain a real competitive advantage.
